item.title: Madame X (Madame Pierre Gautreau)
item.museum: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
item.lesson: Virginie Amélie Gautreau, New Orleans-born and the most theatrical beauty in Paris, was famous for lavender powder and calculated entrances. Sargent, an ambitious young American, was not commissioned; he pursued her for the portrait, betting a sensation at the Salon would crown his Paris career. He got his sensation. As painted in 1884, one jeweled strap of her black dress hung fallen off her shoulder — and Paris erupted. Critics savaged it, crowds mocked, her mother begged him to withdraw the picture; the anonymous title fooled no one. Sargent repainted the strap safely onto the shoulder, but the damage held: he moved to London, where he became the supreme portraitist of the age. He kept the painting in his studio for thirty years, telling the Met when he finally sold it, 'I suppose it is the best thing I have done.' The scandal reads strangely now — the profile like cut marble, the black dress against unearthly skin — because what once looked indecent has resolved into what it always was: pure, cold, magnificent style.
item.lookFor: Look at her right arm's odd torque and the white-knuckled grip on the fan — beneath the statue-still profile, the pose is full of tension. Sargent painted the effort it takes to look effortless.
